title: Chapter 31: Summoning the Sisterhood
content: The circle was waiting for me.

I'd walked the Boundary Stones a hundred times since I first came to Blackwood Vale — tramping through the underbrush with Leo, tracing the perimeter of my family's legacy with my fingers and my frustration — but I had never stood in the center and felt them looking back. Six stones, weathered and moss-gripped, arranged in an imperfect ring at the heart of the forest where the oldest oaks bent inward like they were trying to eavesdrop. The ground around them was bare in a way that had nothing to do with foot traffic and everything to do with the memory of power.
I set Maren's journal at the first stone.

The leather was cracked, the binding so fragile that the spine threatened to crumble if I pressed too hard. Maren Buena, 1766. The first Aurelia. The woman who'd looked at the Hollow Council's trap and stepped into it anyway because someone had to hold the line. Her handwriting was tiny, precise, each letter a small act of defiance against the darkness pressing in.
The stone beneath the journal flickered. A pulse of amber, there and gone, like the last heartbeat of something that had been sleeping for a very long time.

I moved to the second stone. Cordelia. 1803. I placed her journal — fuller than Maren's, bloated with sketches and calculations and desperate, brilliant marginalia — and felt the air shift. It was subtle at first. A dryness in my throat. A prickle along my forearms, static charge building like the atmosphere before a thunderstorm. I swallowed, and the taste of copper sat on my tongue.

Third stone. Elspeth. 1841. Her journal was the heaviest because she'd pressed flowers between every page — pressed them and then written over them, trapping the shriveled blooms beneath her cramped angular script. The flowers had long since disintegrated into ghostly brown shapes, but the smell lingered. Old paper and older grief.
Fourth. Rosamund. 1879. Fifth. Iris. 1917. Sixth. Celine. 1955.

With each journal, the circle tightened. Not physically — the stones didn't move — but the air between them thickened. The charge built. My hair lifted from my shoulders in slow strands, floating as though I were submerged in water. The ground beneath my feet began to hum, a frequency so low I felt it more than heard it, vibrating up through my soles and into my shins, rattling my teeth.
I set Nadine's journal at the seventh position.

There was no seventh stone. The circle had six. Had always had six, for as long as anyone could remember. The gap between the sixth and first stones was wider than the others, a visible absence, a missing tooth in a jaw that had learned to chew around it. I placed my grandmother's journal on the bare earth in that gap and felt the incompleteness like a wound.

The amber glow brightened. All six stones now, pulsing in sequence — first to sixth, first to sixth, like a heart trying to find its rhythm with a chamber missing. The hum climbed a half-step. The static in the air made my eyes water, and the smell hit me: rain and roses, the scent of my grandmother's garden, the scent of every woman who'd tended it before her. It came from the stones themselves, or from the journals, or from the ground under my palms — impossible to tell.
The circle had closed. But it was not complete.
I stood in the center of the pulsing amber light and faced the problem.

Seven ancestresses. Seven gifts, seven journals, seven women whose blood I carried and whose power I'd inherited in fragments. To face the Hollow Council — to have any chance of surviving what was coming — I needed all of them. Not one at a time, not called singly in the desperate scrabble of crisis, but together. Simultaneously. An army of ghosts standing shoulder to shoulder in the space between the stones.
It had never been done.
Not because it was impossible. Because it was forbidden.

The Hollow Council's law, etched into the metaphysical architecture of Blackwood Vale like a load-bearing wall: *Ancestral ghosts cannot manifest in the same space as the Hollow Council.* It was their safeguard, their insurance policy, the rule that ensured no matter how many daughters the Buena line produced, no matter how strong the gift grew, the dead could never assemble. One at a time, the Council could pick us off. They'd been doing it for 260 years.

So I could either lure the Council away from this place — away from the forest, away from the stones — which was laughable; this was their territory, their architecture, and I had nothing to bait them with that they wanted more than they wanted me dead.
Or I could pull them in.

Pull them into the circle. Into a space defined by the Boundary Stones, the journals, and the accumulated power of seven generations of women who'd been waiting, patient and furious, for exactly this moment. A space where the old rules bent. Where maybe — just maybe — *my* rules applied.
The tactical problem chewed at me. How do you summon an army when the enemy's law prevents it? You don't circumvent the law. You break the courtroom.
I was still turning it over when Leo spoke.
"Whatever you're going to do," he said, "do it fast. The fog is moving."

I turned. He stood at the perimeter, a dark silhouette against the amber glow, and in his hands he held the foci — Mrs. Thorne's artifacts, each one a small amplifier tuned to a specific spirit's frequency. A tarnished silver locket for Maren. A brass compass for Cordelia. A dried rose for Elspeth. A child's thimble for Rosamund. A fountain pen for Iris. A silver photograph frame for Celine. And in his left hand, cradled like something precious, Nadine's: a wooden spool, hand-carved, wrapped in a thread so old it had turned the color of smoke.

Leo didn't have magic. He'd never had magic. But he had presence — the kind of steady, unflinching attention that made the stones brighten when he walked past, that made spirits lean toward him like flowers toward light. The circle recognized him. The foci hummed against his palms, their connections strong and clear.

He wasn't looking at me. He was looking east, toward the line where the forest met the vale, and the fog was rolling in. Not natural fog. Not the clean gray mist that crept off the river on autumn mornings. This was the Hollow Council's fog — thick, amber-dark, shot through with something that gleamed like oil on water. It moved with intention. It moved with hunger.
"How long?" I asked.
"Minutes. Maybe less." He shifted his weight, and the foci chimed against each other, a dissonant little bell. "Aurelia. Whatever you're about to do."
"I know."
"Do it fast."

I turned back to the circle. The gap between the sixth and first stones yawned. The pulse of amber light stuttered, arrhythmic, desperate. Six-seven, six-seven, six — and nothing where the seventh should be.
And then Mrs. Thorne arrived.

She came through the trees without preamble, without the careful measured steps I'd come to expect from her. She was running. Mrs. Thorne — who never ran, who treated urgency as a personal failing — was running. Her gray hair had come loose from its pin. Her shawl was caught on a branch she hadn't bothered to untangle. She burst into the clearing and stopped, panting, one hand pressed to her sternum.

"Your grandmother told me," she said, before I could speak, before Leo could turn from his vigil at the perimeter. "Years ago. Before the diminishing took hold — she was still herself, still sharp. She sat in my kitchen and drank my tea and told me things I didn't want to hear, as she always did." Mrs. Thorne's voice was raw, scraped thin by exertion and something older. "She said when the seventh stood at the stones, I should bring the circle."
"What circle?"
Mrs. Thorne reached into the folds of her shawl and produced something that caught the amber light and threw it back tenfold.
A stone.

Not ancient. Not weathered by centuries of rain and root and patient neglect. This stone was small — the size of my fist — and its surface was freshly carved, the marks of a chisel still visible in the grain. The symbol on its face was the same that marked the other six: the Buena spiral, the endless coil that meant *we persist*. But this spiral was different. It had been carved by a hand that trembled. The lines wavered. Not with uncertainty — with effort. With the determination of someone who was running out of time and strength and self, and who chose to spend what remained on this.
Nadine's spiral. Nadine's stone.

"She carved it," Mrs. Thorne said, and her voice broke on the word like a wave on a cliff. "Before the diminishing took the last of her. She sat in the garden with my tools — *my* tools, I didn't know she'd taken them, I didn't know until she was done — and she carved this. A placeholder, she called it. A seat at the table reserved for the seventh." Mrs. Thorne looked at me, and her eyes were bright with tears she would never let fall. "She knew you'd need it. She knew she wouldn't be here to sit in it."
I took the stone.

It was warm. Not sun-warm — there hadn't been sun in Blackwood Vale for days. It was body-warm, as though Nadine had just set it down, as though she'd been holding it moments ago, as though she was still holding it still, her ghostly hands overlapping mine.

The granite was rough against my palm. The carved spiral pressed into the meat below my thumb. The hum in the ground climbed another half-step, anticipatory, eager, like an orchestra waiting for the conductor to raise her baton.

I walked to the gap between the sixth and first stones. The place where the circle had been broken for 260 years. The place where Nadine's journal sat alone on the bare earth, the pulse of amber light shivering around it like a wound that wouldn't close.
I crouched. I placed the seventh stone.
The effect was not subtle.

The amber glow didn't brighten — it *detonated*. A ring of light erupted from the stones, expanding outward in a shockwave that flattened the grass and shook the lower branches of the oaks. The hum became a roar, a frequency I felt in my marrow, in the roots of my teeth, in the wet tissue of my lungs. The ground beneath my feet bucked once, hard, and I stumbled, caught myself, stayed standing through stubbornness alone.

Mrs. Thorne had fallen. She was on her knees in the dead leaves, one hand braced on the earth, the other pressed flat as though she could feel what I felt — the settling of an architecture that had been incomplete for two and a half centuries finally finding its load-bearing wall.

Seven stones. Seven journals. Seven gifts — the foci in Leo's hands, each one flaring now with its own light, Maren's silver, Cordelia's brass, Elspeth's rose, Rosamund's thimble, Iris's pen, Celine's frame, and Nadine's spool, the old thread unspooling in the amber brilliance like a tendril of smoke reaching for the sky.
The circle was complete.

For the first time in 260 years, the Boundary Stones were whole. The ring of amber light was seamless — no gap, no stutter, no arrhythmic heart. The pulse was steady now, strong, a drumbeat that matched the blood in my temples. The smell of rain and roses was so thick I could taste it, sweet and metallic on my tongue, and the air was so charged that every breath sparked against my teeth.

I stood in the center of it, the last Aurelia, the living girl with the dead women's words arranged in a circle around her, and I felt them. All of them. Not summoned yet, not manifested, but *present* — pressing against the edges of the world like hands against glass, waiting for the door to open.

But the door had a lock. The Hollow Council's law. Their safeguard. Their final, absolute insurance that no matter what the Buena women managed, the dead could never stand together in the same space as the Council.
I was going to have to invite the Council in.

The certainty of it settled in my chest like a stone dropping into still water. The only way to summon seven spirits past the Council's law was to make the space where the law didn't apply — or rather, where a different law took precedence. The Boundary Stones defined a space of Buena power. My power. Our power. Inside this circle, I was the authority, not them. If they entered, they entered on my terms.
If they entered.
The alternative was to stand here with seven open seats and no guests, a girl throwing a party for ghosts who couldn't cross the threshold.

The ground shook again. Not the settling hum of the completed circle this time — something else. Something approaching. I felt it in my bones, in the back of my throat, in the place where my gift sat coiled and waiting. A pressure. A weight. The accumulated misery of 260 years of harvested trauma, pressing toward the forest through the fog like a fist pushing through wet clay.
They knew.

The Hollow Council knew what I was doing. They could feel the circle complete — of course they could. They'd built the gap into it. They'd counted on the incompleteness. And now that the seventh stone had been placed, now that the ring had closed for the first time since Maren Buena laid the foundation, they were coming to stop it.

I looked at Leo. He was facing the fog now, both hands full of burning artifacts, his jaw set in a line that meant he wasn't going anywhere. I looked at Mrs. Thorne. She'd risen to her feet, and she was watching me with an expression I couldn't name — pride, maybe, and grief, and the kind of exhaustion that comes from keeping a secret for too long.

I looked at the seventh stone. Nadine's stone. Carved by a hand that was already fading, by a woman who knew she would not survive to see this moment but who made the preparations anyway. Who left me a seat at the table she couldn't sit in. Who trusted me to do what she could not.

The fog reached the first line of oaks. The amber light from the stones flared brighter, and within the fog I could see shapes — not faces yet, not bodies, but the suggestion of them. The Council didn't travel alone. They brought their harvest with them: every scrap of anguish they'd collected, every stolen memory, every echo of Buena suffering they'd fed on for two and a half centuries. It moved behind them like a tide.

Seven stones. Seven journals. Seven women waiting on the other side of the glass. One living girl standing in the middle of a 260-year-old circle, trying to summon the dead to fight the architects of her family's suffering.
What could go wrong?
